frameworks.name: functional localization (fMRI)
frameworks.one_line: identify cortical areas selectively activated by surface glossiness

frameworks.name: perceptual constancy
frameworks.one_line: leverages glossiness constancy across illumination to dissociate from low-level cues

frameworks.name: selective attention
frameworks.one_line: task-driven attention to glossiness vs form/orientation isolates gloss-selective activation

core_question: Which cortical areas underlie human perception of surface glossiness, independent of low-level luminance cues?
key_assumptions: glossiness perception is constant across illumination changes
low-level luminance and luminance-contrast can be dissociated from gloss
manipulations: object surface (glossy vs matte) under bright vs dim illumination
selective attention to glossiness vs 3D form vs 3D orientation
key_findings: regions responded more to glossy-dim than matte-bright stimuli despite lower luminance
regions responded more during glossiness discrimination than form or orientation tasks
bilateral ventral occipital areas are common across both experiments
interpretations: bilateral ventral occipital cortex is involved in glossiness perception independent of low-level luminance features
